title: Naive homology versus Suslin homology
speaker: Fabien Morel
tags: Workshop on Chow Groups Motives and Derived Categories

proof_stages.label: Naive algebraic singular complex
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a base field, defines the algebraic simplices Delta^n, the naive algebraic singular simplicial set S_bullet(X)=Hom(Delta^bullet,X) and its sheaf version, and then defines naive homology by taking ordinary chains on that simplicial set.

proof_stages.label: Elementary computations exposing naivety
proof_stages.summary: A sequence of computations shows that the naive singular complex is either trivial or very hard to compute: finite 2tale schemes, A^1, G_m, A^1-rigid varieties, P^1, P^n, and P^infty illustrate its range.

proof_stages.label: Canonical comparison with Suslin homology
proof_stages.summary: Suslin homology is recalled as the homology of the simplicial abelian group of finite correspondences from algebraic simplices, giving a canonical map from naive homology to Suslin homology whose behavior in examples motivates imposing strong A^1-connectedness hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Mod-l rigidity theorem for homogeneous spaces
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that over an algebraically closed field, for l different from the characteristic and for suitable smooth homogeneous spaces X=G/H with G semisimple simply connected and H connected reductive, the mod-l comparison from naive to Suslin homology is an isomorphism, equivalently to 2tale homology via Suslin-Voevodsky.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to the group case and A^1-goodness
proof_stages.summary: Using triviality of H-torsors over affine spaces and known results that the A^1-singular construction on split semisimple simply connected groups is already A^1-local, the proof reduces the homogeneous-space theorem to a zero-connected simplicial group attached to G.

proof_stages.label: Curtis lower-central-series devissage
proof_stages.summary: Passing to the classifying space of the zero-connected simplicial group gives a one-connected object, so the Curtis spectral sequence from the lower central series of a free simplicial-group model can be used to reduce rigidity questions to abelian chain-complex pieces involving free Lie algebra functors.

proof_stages.label: Cells, transfers, and Suslin-Voevodsky rigidity
proof_stages.summary: A Bruhat-type cell decomposition reduces the relevant pieces to A^1-sphere-like complexes, these are replaced by A^1-equivalent complexes with transfers, and Suslin-Voevodsky rigidity is then applied to conclude the desired mod-l rigidity and recover the naive homology statement.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with stable proof and possible extension
proof_stages.summary: The speaker contrasts the unstable proof with the usual motivic/Suslin proof and ends by proposing punctured affine spaces as a concrete test case for stronger integral or cartesian-square refinements.

examples.name: Multiplicative group G_m
examples.note: Since maps from A^1 to G_m are constant, naive homology has H_0 freely generated by k^*, and its failure to match Suslin homology is used to justify excluding toric factors by imposing semisimplicity.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses A^1-rigidity of G_m to compute the simplicial set as constant, computes H_0 as the free abelian group on k^*, and compares it to Suslin homology

passes.reason: the split torus is homogeneous but A^1-rigid so the naive complex is discrete, and it is used to show theta is not an isomorphism

examples.name: A^1-rigid curves and abelian varieties
examples.note: Curves of genus at least one and abelian varieties have only constant A^1-simplices, so their naive homology is just the free group on rational points while Suslin H_0 is CH_0.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the same A^1-rigidity computation to curves of genus at least one and abelian varieties, then discusses the induced map from the free group on rational points to CH_0

passes.reason: A^1-rigidity forces only constant A^1-simplices, and abelian-variety cases are used to show naive homology differs from CH_0/Suslin homology

examples.name: Projective line and projective spaces
examples.note: For P^1 the first naive homology is K_1^MW, an extension of k^* by I^2, while the comparison to Suslin homology kills I^2; this both motivates the algebraically closed hypothesis and shows the theorem in its first nontrivial case.
examples.engagement_reason: he gives concrete homotopy and homology information for P^1 and P^n, including the H_1 formula, the k^* result for higher projective spaces, and the comparison map to Suslin homology

passes.reason: projective spaces are maximally homogeneous, and P^1 is used over general fields to exhibit the I^2-kernel obstructing isomorphism
Projective spaces are maximally homogeneous, and P1 with K1^MW -> k^* and kernel I^2 obstructs a general isomorphism over arbitrary fields.
